mnemonics.title: Binary Search Steps
mnemonics.content: FIND MIDDLE → COMPARE → ELIMINATE HALF → REPEAT. For binary search: always calculate mid = low + (high-low)/2 to avoid overflow.

mnemonics.title: Sorting Algorithm Patterns
mnemonics.content: STABLE sorts: Merge, Insertion, Bubble, Counting, Radix. UNSTABLE: Quick, Selection, Heap. Stable means equal values keep their original order.

mnemonics.title: Time Complexity Memory
mnemonics.content: QUICK: O(n log n) average, O(n²) worst. MERGE: O(n log n) always. HEAP: O(n log n) always. BUBBLE/SELECTION/INSERTION: O(n²). For small data, INSERTION SORT beats others!

mnemonics.title: Divide and Conquer Algo List
mnemonics.content: DIVIDE: Break problem into subproblems. CONQUER: Solve subproblems recursively. COMBINE: Merge solutions. Algorithms: Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication.

mnemonics.title: When to Use Which Sort
mnemonics.content: SMALL DATA (<50): Insertion Sort. ALMOST SORTED: Insertion Sort or Bubble Sort. LARGE DATA: Quick Sort or Merge Sort. INTEGER DATA (bounded): Counting/Radix Sort. GUARANTEED PERFORMANCE: Heap Sort or Merge Sort.

acronyms.usage: Enables O(log n) search, insert, delete in balanced case. In-order traversal gives sorted order.
Describes worst-case complexity. O(n²) means algorithm never takes worse than n² operations.
Describes best-case complexity. Ω(n) for linear search means at least n comparisons needed.
Describes average-case when upper and lower bounds match. Θ(n log n) for Merge Sort in all cases.
Quick Sort is in-place (O(log n) for recursion stack). Merge Sort needs O(n) extra space.

visualTips.visualization: Think of searching for a word in a dictionary. Open middle, decide which half contains word, repeat. The search space shrinks by HALF each step - draw a binary tree! Number of steps = log₂(n).

Imagine partitioning books by genre. Pick one book as PIVOT (anchor). Move all books BEFORE it if smaller genre, AFTER if larger genre. PIVOT ends in correct sorted position. Recursively sort left and right groups.
Two sorted card piles on table. Always pick SMALLER of top cards from each pile and place in result pile. Continue until one pile empty, then dump rest. Result is fully sorted!

Draw binary tree where each parent is LARGER than children (max-heap). Root = maximum element. For sorting: repeatedly remove root and heapify remaining. Array representation: parent at i, children at 2i+1 and 2i+2.
Linear: Check each person in a line one by one. Binary: Ask 'Are you taller than average?' then eliminate half. Binary needs SORTED data! Linear works on unsorted data.

analogies.analogy: Searching for a contact in unsorted phone by scrolling from A-Z one by one. Simple but slow for large lists.
Looking up a word in dictionary. You don't flip page by page - you open to middle, then quarter, then eighth, halving each time. Requires sorted dictionary!
Organizing books on shelf by height. Pick a book as reference height. Move all shorter books to left, taller to right. Reference book finds its final spot. Repeat for left and right groups.
Sorting playing cards: Deal cards into piles (divide). Sort each pile individually (conquer). Merge two sorted piles by taking smaller top card each time (combine).
Sorting playing cards in your hand. Pick next card, insert into correct position among already-sorted cards. Like arranging cards when you receive them one by one.
Finding minimum: Scan entire list, swap with first position. Find next minimum: Scan remaining, swap with second position. Like selecting smallest, then next smallest, then next.
Bubbles rising: Compare adjacent pairs, swap if wrong order. Largest elements 'bubble up' to end. Like arranging people by height - repeatedly compare neighbors and swap if out of order.

Priority queue: Imagine a stack of papers where each paper has priority number. Always take top (maximum) paper, then reorganize remaining. Repeat. Uses binary heap property where parent is always larger than children.
